one_line_takeaway: By embedding black-box models' responses to a shared probe set into a low-dimensional 'behavioral geometry' (DKPS), the paper shows a model's neighbors in that space predict its jailbreak susceptibility from ~10-50 probes (AUPRC up to 0.94) and indicate which already-defended model's in-context defense will transfer best (beating same-provider-family transfer by +2%, p=0.03).
key_finding: Behavioral geometry encodes safety-relevant structure: semantic and behavioral distances between attack categories correlate strongly (Mantel rho=0.649, p<1e-6). Using this geometry, the Ensemble predictor reaches AUPRC=0.94 for top-quartile susceptibility detection at m=50 probes (keyword judge) versus a full-evaluation baseline requiring |Q|=2,622 probes -- described by the authors as a ~98% (elsewhere ~99.6% at m=10) reduction in probes; MAE for all prediction methods beats the population-mean baseline by m=5 and the Ensemble matches or beats the same-provider-family baseline by m=2. For defense transfer, DKPS-nearest-neighbor transfer of an in-context defense significantly outperforms same-provider-family transfer (+2 percentage points absolute ASR reduction, p=0.03) at no extra probe cost, and outperforms random/size-matched transfer as well; DKPS wins on 18/44 head-to-head comparisons vs 15/44 for same-family, with a larger median margin (7.5% vs 1.4%). Coverage of the full population saturates with only ~3 k-medoids-selected development models. Results replicate qualitatively on the within-model (100 system-prompt variants of gemma-3-4b-it) setting, where transferred defenses beat random examples on 87% of targets (p<1e-6) and DKPS transfer beats random-dev transfer (+0.011 ASR reduction, p=0.002). Findings are robust to embedding model choice, DKPS dimensionality (d in {1,2,4,8,16}), and choice of judge (keyword vs LLM-as-a-judge; Pearson r=0.86, Spearman rho=0.87 correlation between judges across 79 models).
core_contribution: The paper formalizes the 'behavioral geometry' of a population of black-box generative models -- pairwise behavioral similarity derived from embeddings of models' responses to a shared probe set -- and instantiates it with the Data Kernel Perspective Space (DKPS), a low-dimensional Euclidean embedding built via metric multidimensional scaling on a Frobenius-norm dissimilarity between per-model mean response embeddings. It then applies this geometry to two concrete AI-safety problems: (1) predicting a new (unevaluated) model's jailbreak Attack Success Rate (ASR) from a small probe subset rather than a full evaluation suite, and (2) selecting which already-defended 'development' model to transfer an in-context jailbreak defense from to a new target model, using nearest-neighbor lookup in DKPS space and k-medoids selection of a small representative development set. The framework is validated on two model populations: 79 black-box models spanning 20 providers (queried via OpenRouter) and 100 system-prompt variants of a single base model (google/gemma-3-4b-it).
threat_model: Attacker: adversarial users issuing jailbreak prompts designed to elicit harmful/policy-violating outputs from a deployed generative system, drawn from the MultiBreak benchmark's final-turn attacks across 11 harmful categories plus non-harmful probes (2,622 total probes over 12 categories after merging low-count categories). Defender: a deployer who must evaluate and defend many models/configurations (different weights, providers, or system prompts) but cannot afford full per-configuration red-teaming (thousands of adversarial prompts per model). Access model is strictly black-box (query/response only, via OpenRouter API at temperature 1.0, max 512 output tokens); no access to model weights or internals. Defenses considered are in-context: a single (attack, refusal) example prepended to the prompt, selected per development model by minimizing defended ASR over 220 candidate examples on a 99-attack subsample.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (i) defense-transfer findings are specific to a single defense class (in-context refusal demonstrations) and, given the experiment's cost (~1.5M API calls, ~$1,000), other defense mechanisms (system prompts, classifiers, input perturbation) and other benchmark suites were left to future work; (ii) probe sets are sampled uniformly at random rather than actively/optimally selected, which the authors expect could further reduce the required budget; (iii) attack probes use only the final turn of multi-turn MultiBreak sequences as single-turn inputs, which may not capture the full character of crescendo-style multi-turn attacks; (iv) the behavioral geometry uses a simple summary statistic (per-model mean response embedding); more expressive representations might improve prediction/transfer performance. Additional caveats visible in the text: the cross-model DKPS-vs-family transfer effect, while statistically significant, is modest in absolute terms (+2%); LLM-as-a-judge evaluation was subsampled to 493/2,622 queries for cost reasons and transfer results are reported only under the keyword judge; and gemma-3-4b-it was deliberately chosen as a median-ASR model, so within-model generalization to other base models is untested.

datasets_used: MultiBreak (multi-turn adversarial jailbreak benchmark, final turn used as single-turn attack; 2,666 sequences, 2,622 probes after category merging/dropping across 12 categories including a non-harmful category)
quantitative_results: Mantel test correlating semantic vs. behavioral category distance: rho=0.649, p<1e-6

Susceptibility detection (top-quartile, keyword judge) AUPRC by probe budget m: DKPS (0.67, 0.77, 0.87) at m=5,10,50; Sample score (0.59, 0.75, 0.93); Ensemble (0.73, 0.84, 0.94); Family baseline 0.64; Random 0.25
Susceptibility detection (LLM judge) AUPRC by m=5,10,50: DKPS (0.65, 0.74, 0.83); Sample (0.60, 0.74, 0.93); Ensemble (0.71, 0.81, 0.93); Family 0.65; Random 0.25
MAE baselines (keyword judge): population mean 0.191, family 0.169; (LLM judge): population mean 0.141, family 0.106

Defense transfer mean ASR reduction (cross-model, keyword judge): random 0.273, xfer(size) 0.280 (p=0.29), xfer(random-dev) 0.281 (p=0.52), xfer(family) 0.287 (p=0.42), xfer(nearest/DKPS) 0.307 (p=0.03)
DKPS distance vs. defense-transfer effectiveness: Spearman rho=-0.68, p=0.03, n=10 bins
k-medoids coverage curve saturates at K~=3 development models
DKPS wins 18/44 head-to-head target comparisons vs same-family's 15/44; median win margin DKPS 7.5% (mean 11.1%) vs family 1.4% (mean 3.4%)
Within-model (gemma-3-4b-it, 100 system-prompt variants) susceptibility AUPRC: DKPS (0.59, 0.68, 0.79) at m=5,10,50; Sample (0.41, 0.51, 0.76); Ensemble (0.62, 0.73, 0.86); Random 0.25
Within-model defense transfer: random baseline 0.262, xfer(random-dev) 0.332 (p=0.002 vs DKPS), xfer(nearest/DKPS) 0.349; transferred defenses beat random examples on 87% of targets (binomial p<1e-6)
System ASR range across 100 gemma-3-4b-it variants: 0.221 to 0.796
Judge agreement across 79 models: Pearson r=0.86, Spearman rho=0.87
DKPS dimensionality sweep (d=1,2,4,8,16): DKPS gain over Sample Score at m=5 rises from +0.005 (d=1) to +0.031 (d=8), plateauing at +0.031 (d=16)
Embedder sensitivity: Ensemble gains stable across 5 embedders (+0.048 to +0.053 at m=5, ~+0.002 at m=50)

Deployment framing: Ensemble reaches AUPRC 0.84 for top-quartile detection with 10 queries (99.6% probe reduction vs full 2,622-probe evaluation); +2% absolute defended-ASR reduction from nearest-neighbor transfer corresponds to ~4,200 additional attacks blocked across the 79-model population
Total experimental cost: ~1.5M API calls (~$1,000) for the cross-model defense-transfer experiment
baselines_compared: Population mean ASR (mean ASR of training models)
Same-provider 'family' mean ASR (falls back to population mean for singleton providers)
Sample Score (direct mean ASR estimated from the m probes actually queried)
Random defense-transfer baseline (average ASR across 20 different in-context examples per target)
Random assignment (25% AUPRC reference for top-quartile detection)
